design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate horizontally-scrolling stages, select optimal weapons from power-ups, manage ship speed settings, destroy five planetary cloaking devices then infiltrate ORN headquarters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing aggressive weapon selection against vulnerability when losing equipped weapons on destruction; managing screen space navigation with large detailed enemies and complex backgrounds
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Piloting the FIRE LEO-03 Styx, a small but heavily-armed starfighter capable of stealth infiltration and tactical strikes against an overwhelming empire
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Definitive shift from overhead free-directional stages (Thunder Force I/II) to horizontal scrolling format, establishing this as the series standard. Weapon system design emphasizes meaningful choice and consequence—losing only current weapon on default difficulty creates risk/reward tension around equipment selection. Speed setting introduces dynamic difficulty adjustment at player discretion.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system with ship destruction penalties
difficulty.continues_system: Continue system present (specific implementation not documented)
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; player can select which of five initial planets to tackle first, providing non-linear stage order
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Significant agency through multiple vectors: four-level adjustable ship speed setting allows real-time difficulty tuning; difficulty mode selection (default vs. higher modes) affects weapon retention mechanics; initial stage selection order provides tactical flexibility

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon power-up system with reusable weapons from Thunder Force II (Twin Shot, Back Shot) plus new exclusive weapons. CLAW support items grant automated companion fire mimicking primary weapon. Speed setting upgradeable across four levels.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Five initial selectable planets (Hydra, Gorgon, Seiren, Haides, Ellis) must be completed to unlock three final stages leading to ORN headquarters. Stage order determined by player choice rather than forced sequence.
replayability_hooks: Multiple difficulty modes with different mechanical consequences (all weapons lost on higher difficulties)
Stage selection order allows different progression paths
Weapon variety encourages experimentation across playthroughs
Speed setting customization enables personal difficulty tuning

world_building_techniques: Pre-game narrative context: 100-year timespan continuation from Thunder Force I/II, establishing Galaxy Federation vs. ORN Empire conflict
Mission briefing premise: Five cloaking devices conceal ORN base; Cerberus defense system requires small, heavily-armed Styx craft
Final boss concept: Cha Os bio-computer as ORN emperor
Enemy design and stage environments implicitly convey military/technological setting

boss_design.philosophy: Large, detailed enemy sprites serving as visual spectacle and mechanical skill tests. Emphasis on pattern recognition and weapon selection strategy against distinct threat types per stage.
notable_bosses: Cha Os (ORN bio-computer emperor, final stage threat)
Stage-specific planetary guardians (names not documented in sources)

faction_indicators: Galaxy Federation (player faction) - technologically advanced, defensive strategy, creates Styx craft
ORN Empire (antagonist faction) - aggressive expansionist power with cloaking technology and Cerberus defense network
Cerberus system - impersonal automated threat, represents technological military escalation
danger_signals: Five planets with active cloaking devices indicate military occupation
Cerberus defense network effectiveness against large ships shows technological threat sophistication
ORN Emperor (Cha Os) as bio-computer suggests threat is distributed, not singular
Need for small stealth craft (Styx) indicates player disadvantage in direct military engagement
environmental_storytelling: Large, detailed enemy sprites convey industrial/military aesthetic
Intricate backgrounds establish lived-in, technologically advanced worlds
Stage-to-stage progression through five different planets with distinct visual environments
Parallax scrolling depth creates sense of vast, complex military infrastructure
